items.myth: The best data scientist picks charts by aesthetic preference or library defaults.
items.reality: Effective EDA starts with the analytical question and variable types; chart family is a logical consequence, not a style choice.
items.example: Class imbalance is a bar-chart question regardless of whether Seaborn countplot or Plotly px.bar draws it.

items.myth: Scatter plots work for any two columns, including target vs row index.
items.reality: Scatter encodes relationship between two meaningful numeric variables; plotting against arbitrary index or ID does not answer a distribution or trend question.
items.example: Residual distribution → histogram of residuals, not scatter(residuals, range(n)).

items.myth: Heatmaps and scatter plots are interchangeable for correlation exploration.
items.reality: Heatmaps summarize an entire matrix at once; scatter inspects one bivariate relationship in detail. Use both at different stages, not instead of each other.
items.example: df.corr() heatmap for screening; ax.scatter(col_a, col_b) for confirming a nonlinear pattern in one pair.

items.myth: Box plots show everything a histogram shows, so histograms are redundant.
items.reality: Box plots compress to quartiles and outliers; they hide multimodality and skew detail that histograms reveal for scaler and transformation choices.
items.example: A bimodal feature looks like one wide box; histogram shows two peaks suggesting a missing latent category or mixture.
